post.title: 13 Years of Transfer Data: What's Really Changed

tweets.text:
13 Years of Transfer Data: What's Really Changed

Football transfers have become an increasingly central part of the game. Every summer window, the conversation shifts to who the next big signing will be and which clubs are willing to break the bank...

Thread 🧵
3,000+ transfers in 2025 alone — up 58% from 2013. Volume has never been higher. But the real story isn't the numbers. It's the money.
~19% of transfers involve a fee. Just 1 in 5. Despite all the headlines, that ratio hasn't changed in 13 years. Free transfers and loans still run the market.
380 → 693 paid transfers from 2013 to 2025. An 82% increase. More clubs are writing bigger checks, more often.
Average fee: €5.7M → €12.7M. But the median only moved €3M → €6.5M. A few mega-deals skew the numbers — most transfers are still modest.
In 2013, two-thirds of paid transfers were under €5M. By 2025, barely half. The €30M+ tier grew from 3.4% to 11.4%. What used to be elite is becoming routine.
13 transfers broke €30M in 2013. In 2025? 79. A 6x increase in a decade. The top end of the market has been completely transformed.
The €30-50M range: 10 → 49 deals per year. The €50-80M tier: 1 → 25. The truly elite (€80M+) remains rare — but everything below it has normalized.
From Bale and Neymar in 2013 to Isak and Wirtz in 2025 — the names change. The clubs signing them? Rarely do.
€3.8 billion. That's what the Premier League spent in 2025 — more than 3x Serie A, 5x La Liga. England doesn't just lead the market. It is the market.
25 clubs out of 534 control ~52% of all transfer spending — every single year since 2013. The window changes. The names change. The share doesn't.
Chelsea (€3.2B), Man City (€2.4B), Man United (€2.4B), PSG (€2.3B). The same clubs keep writing the biggest checks. The transfer market's oligarchy is alive and well.
Real Madrid, PSG, Man City, Chelsea — the same logos, every window. And one new entrant impossible to ignore: the Saudi clubs.
